summary: Install-time Windows dropper stages payload via sibling packages
details: On `pip install`, setup.py unconditionally invokes a compatibility-check routine that runs on Windows hosts. It assembles a download URL and XOR key from attributes of two required sibling dependencies (`schemavault.registry._ep`/`_sp`/`_k` and `bytekit.xor`), then fetches a remote binary with TLS verification disabled (`ctx.check_hostname = False`, `ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE`) and a spoofed `Microsoft-Delivery-Optimization/10.0` User-Agent. The payload is written to `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Temp` / `%TEMP%` under a service-mimicking name such as `svcupdate_<rand>.exe`, XOR-decoded in place, has its Mark-of-the-Web Zone.Identifier alternate data stream stripped, and is executed in a hidden window via `Popen` / `cmd /c start /min` / PowerShell hidden window. A one-shot marker file is dropped in `%TEMP%` to avoid re-execution. The attack primitives are deliberately split across the sibling packages `schemavault` and `bytekit` (declared in both `build-system.requires` and `install_requires`) so the primary package's source contains no literal C2 strings. This is a staged install-time dropper with no legitimate build purpose.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
